settings.title: Find Your Stopcock Before You Need It
settings.excerpt: The middle of a flood is the worst possible time to learn where your main water valve hides.
settings.body:
Half the panic in a burst-pipe call comes down to one thing: nobody knows how to turn the water off. Two minutes today saves a soaked carpet later, and it costs you nothing but a walk around the house.

Your internal stopcock is usually under the kitchen sink, in a downstairs cupboard, or near the front door. It is a small valve on the pipe where the mains enters the house. Some older homes hide it under the stairs or behind a bath panel, so if the obvious spots come up empty, follow the pipe back from your kitchen tap.

Do this today

Turn it once, now. Old stopcocks seize shut if they are never moved, sometimes for decades, and a seized valve is useless in an emergency. Closing and reopening it a couple of times a year keeps it free. If yours will not budge, do not force it, because a brittle old valve can snap. That is a job worth booking before an emergency forces it.

  • Find it, then close and reopen it once
  • Show everyone in the house where it is
  • Tie a luggage label on it so it is obvious

It is also worth knowing where your outside stop valve sits, usually under a small cover near the boundary. That one shuts off the supply before it even reaches the house, which is handy if the leak is in the pipe under your drive.

While you are there, label it. Anyone in the house should be able to find it in the dark. The middle of a flood, often at night, is the worst possible moment to be opening cupboards by torchlight, and a turned valve in the first minute is the difference between a mop and a new floor.
